symbols: golden spear or arrow — divine love piercing the soul, the transverberation described by Teresa herself
angel's smile — the sweetness of divine encounter, love that wounds and heals simultaneously
Teresa's swooning posture — the total surrender of the self to God in mystical union
billowing drapery — the spiritual turbulence and ecstasy of the soul ravished by divine love
golden rays of light descending — the divine grace and glory pouring down from heaven
architectural aedicula with columns — the altar-like setting, framing the mystical event as a liturgical revelation
description: A photograph of Bernini's famous sculptural group in the Cornaro Chapel, Santa Maria della Vittoria, Rome. Saint Teresa of Avila reclines on a cloud of marble, her body arched backward, eyes closed, mouth slightly open in an expression of overwhelming spiritual ecstasy. Her Carmelite habit billows in deeply carved folds. Above her, a smiling youthful angel holds a golden spear poised to pierce her heart. Golden metal rays descend from above, simulating divine light.
theological_description: Bernini's Ecstasy of Saint Teresa (1647-1652) is the supreme visual realization of Christian mystical theology. It depicts the transverberation described in Teresa's autobiography: 'I saw in his hand a long spear of gold, and at the iron's point there seemed to be a little fire. He appeared to me to be thrusting it at times into my heart, and to pierce my very entrails; when he drew it out, he seemed to draw them out also, and to leave me all on fire with a great love of God.' This experience of divine love as simultaneously painful and blissful stands in the tradition of the Song of Solomon (Song 2:5: 'I am sick with love') and the mystical theology of John of the Cross. The angel's golden spear evokes the flaming sword of the cherubim at Eden's gate (Genesis 3:24), now transformed from an instrument of exclusion to one of intimate divine encounter. Teresa's ecstasy is not escapism but the fullest engagement of the whole person -- body, mind, and spirit -- with the living God, fulfilling the great commandment to love the Lord 'with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might' (Deuteronomy 6:5). Bernini's theatrical staging within the chapel, with the Cornaro family observing from side balconies, insists that mystical experience is not private but ecclesial -- the saints' encounters with God are given for the edification of the whole Church. The golden rays descending from above recall Psalm 36:9: 'In your light do we see light.'

scripture_refs.reason: He brought me to the banqueting house, and his banner over me was love; I am sick with love -- the bridal mysticism of Teresa's experience.
Love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and might -- the total self-giving depicted in Teresa's ecstasy.
Paul caught up to the third heaven, hearing things that cannot be told -- the apostolic precedent for mystical rapture.
In your light do we see light -- the golden rays of divine illumination descending upon the saint.
The flaming sword of the cherubim, here transformed into the angel's spear of divine love that opens rather than bars the way to God.
